title: Meta launched a new AI optimism ad set to a song about human extinction
excerpt: David Bowie's song "Five Years," which Meta used in a supposedly inspiring advertisement, is about humans learning that they have five years left to live before the apocalypse.
content_html:
Meta’s newest advertisement begins with a black-and-white shot of an eye, showing us what someone sees as they read countless panicked headlines about how AI is going to take our jobs, isolate us, and spark a global crisis.

“Some people will have you believe AI is going to make us feel less connected. That it’s going to leave us behind,” a voiceover says. “We couldn’t disagree more.”

Suddenly, the video shifts to color, and shows a cycle of different people opening their eyes and smiling. Then, we see a couple dancing on a rooftop, pointing at a rainbow; a group of teens swimming in a lake; a child frolicking in a field; friends embracing after time apart.

“Call us optimists. Call us dreamers. Call us whatever the hell you want. But we’re betting on people, and we like those odds,” the voiceover says. “The future is for everyone.”

That’s a nice sentiment — as well as pretty convenient for a company betting hundreds of billions of dollars that AI will revolutionize humanity. But the strangest part of the advertisement is not that we’re watching these happy moments play out via Instagram posts. It’s that the soundtrack to the ad is the David Bowie song “Five Years.”

If you are not familiar with this song, I urge you to give it a listen, read the lyrics, and think about what it is trying to say. It seems clear to me, but I studied poetry in college, so as a control for this experiment, I asked my brother — a blockchain analyst who loves Claude Code and does not read for fun — if he could tell me what the song is about.

“I thought climate change at first, then zombie apocalypse, then an asteroid hitting the earth,” he told me. He is correct. It is a song about the human race panicking after learning they will die in a mass-extinction event in five years.

If you’re not familiar with Bowie’s music, this track might sound happy and inspiring, matching the ad’s upbeat tone. The part of the song that is used for the advertisement was probably chosen because it uses the word “people” over and over again, and without the context of the song, it’s not clear what it’s about.

But if we look at the lines directly preceding this section:

News had just come over
We had five years left to cry in
News guy wept and told us
Earth was really dying
Cried so much his face was wet
Then I knew he was not lying

We “had five years left to cry in” and the “earth was really dying.” It’s pretty bleak.

It is not reassuring to convince people that AI is going to make the world better while playing a song about the end of the world, and yet, this contradictory musical choice seems to have sailed right past everyone at Meta, including CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

“Meta has always believed in giving people the power to share, connect, and shape your world in the ways you want,” he wrote alongside the video. “As we enter this next wave with AI, we continue to believe the future is for everyone. We’re focused on giving every person the tools to reach your full potential and making sure the benefits of technology are distributed to everyone.”

Then again, tech leaders are not known for their literary analysis skills. Meta’s Oculus used to give new hires copies of the science fiction novel “Ready Player One,” which is set in a dystopia in which a tech company making virtual reality products becomes overly powerful and evil. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has directly cited inspiration from the movie “Her,” which warns us about what can go wrong when we use AI for emotional support. Palantir, a company that builds AI surveillance systems for the government, is named after Palantir, a seeing stone from the “Lord of the Rings” franchise that the Dark Lord uses to spy on his enemies. Elon Musk is currently throwing a fit about the “accuracy” of Christopher Nolan’s blockbuster adaptation of “The Odyssey,” a story with such realistic elements as sea monsters, magic, and divine intervention. These guys make a great argument for the value of studying the humanities.

Meta isn’t alone in its recent promotional foibles. Instead of racing to build AGI, the top AI companies seem to be fighting over who can make the creepiest advertisement. A few weeks ago, Anthropic released an eerie video of its own. As my colleague Lucas Ropek described it:

The ad begins with a video of a burning house (not exactly a heartwarming start) before pivoting to a series of still images. These images include a crowd of people being surveilled by facial recognition, a homeless person sleeping on the street, rows upon rows of tombstones in a cemetery, and what appears to be a group of laborers toiling in a mine where (presumably) raw materials for smartphones are being dug up.

Meanwhile, a voice-over track features different people asking questions like “Can AI be trusted?” and “Who’s gonna hit the brakes if we need to?”

Anthropic is trying to convince us that it understands the risks AI poses to society, and therefore, this is the company that people can trust to develop AI responsibly. The message it actually conveys feels closer to the mood of Bowie’s “Five Years.”

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman responded to the Anthropic ad, “I thought this was satire, kept looking for the handle to be spelled c1audeai or something.”

As these companies spar to control the public perception of AI, their efforts don’t seem to be making much progress. A recent Pew survey found that only 16% of Americans think that AI’s impact on society over the next 20 years will be positive, and 40% believe it will have a negative impact. Better luck next time, Meta.

When you purchase through links in our articles, we may earn a small commission. This doesn’t affect our editorial independence.

Amanda Silberling is a senior writer at TechCrunch covering the intersection of technology and culture. She has also written for publications like Polygon, MTV, the Kenyon Review, NPR, and Business Insider. She is the co-host of Wow If True, a podcast about internet culture, with science fiction author Isabel J. Kim. Prior to joining TechCrunch, she worked as a grassroots organizer, museum educator, and film festival coordinator. She holds a B.A. in English from the University of Pennsylvania and served as a Princeton in Asia Fellow in Laos.

You can contact or verify outreach from Amanda by emailing amanda@techcrunch.com or via encrypted message at @amanda.100 on Signal.

blocks.html_en: Meta’s newest advertisement begins with a black-and-white shot of an eye, showing us what someone sees as they read countless panicked headlines about how AI is going to take our jobs, isolate us, and spark a global crisis.

sentences.en: Meta’s newest advertisement begins with a black-and-white shot of an eye, showing us what someone sees as they read countless panicked headlines about how AI is going to take our jobs, isolate us, and spark a global crisis.
sentences.zh: Meta 的最新广告以黑白镜头开始，向我们展示了人们在阅读无数关于人工智能将如何抢走我们的工作、孤立我们并引发全球危机的恐慌头条新闻时所看到的情况。

blocks.html_en: “Some people will have you believe AI is going to make us feel less connected. That it’s going to leave us behind,” a voiceover says. “We couldn’t disagree more.”

sentences.en: “Some people will have you believe AI is going to make us feel less connected.
sentences.zh: “有些人会让你相信人工智能会让我们感觉联系更少。

sentences.en: That it’s going to leave us behind,” a voiceover says.
sentences.zh: 它会把我们抛在后面，”画外音说道。

sentences.en: “We couldn’t disagree more.”
sentences.zh: “我们完全不同意。”

blocks.html_en: Suddenly, the video shifts to color, and shows a cycle of different people opening their eyes and smiling. Then, we see a couple dancing on a rooftop, pointing at a rainbow; a group of teens swimming in a lake; a child frolicking in a field; friends embracing after time apart.

sentences.en: Suddenly, the video shifts to color, and shows a cycle of different people opening their eyes and smiling.
sentences.zh: 突然，视频变成了彩色，并显示了不同的人睁开眼睛微笑的循环。

sentences.en: Then, we see a couple dancing on a rooftop, pointing at a rainbow; a group of teens swimming in a lake; a child frolicking in a field; friends embracing after time apart.
sentences.zh: 然后，我们看到一对夫妇在屋顶上跳舞，指着彩虹；一群青少年在湖里游泳；一个孩子在田野里嬉戏；朋友们分开一段时间后拥抱。

blocks.html_en: “Call us optimists. Call us dreamers. Call us whatever the hell you want. But we’re betting on people, and we like those odds,” the voiceover says. “The future is for everyone.”

sentences.en: “Call us optimists.
sentences.zh: “我们是乐观主义者。

sentences.en: Call us dreamers.
sentences.zh: 请称我们为梦想家。

sentences.en: Call us whatever the hell you want.
sentences.zh: 不管你想要什么，给我们打电话。

sentences.en: But we’re betting on people, and we like those odds,” the voiceover says.
sentences.zh: 但我们把赌注押在人身上，我们喜欢这些赔率，”画外音说道。

sentences.en: “The future is for everyone.”
sentences.zh: “未来属于每个人。”

blocks.html_en: That’s a nice sentiment — as well as pretty convenient for a company betting hundreds of billions of dollars that AI will revolutionize humanity. But the strangest part of the advertisement is not that we’re watching these happy moments play out via Instagram posts. It’s that the soundtrack to the ad is the David Bowie song “Five Years.”

sentences.en: That’s a nice sentiment — as well as pretty convenient for a company betting hundreds of billions of dollars that AI will revolutionize humanity.
sentences.zh: 这是一种很好的情绪，对于一家投入数千亿美元押注人工智能将彻底改变人类的公司来说也相当方便。

sentences.en: But the strangest part of the advertisement is not that we’re watching these happy moments play out via Instagram posts.
sentences.zh: 但广告中最奇怪的部分并不是我们通过 Instagram 帖子观看这些快乐的时刻。

sentences.en: It’s that the soundtrack to the ad is the David Bowie song “Five Years.”
sentences.zh: 广告的配乐是大卫·鲍伊的歌曲《五年》。

blocks.html_en: If you are not familiar with this song, I urge you to give it a listen, read the lyrics, and think about what it is trying to say. It seems clear to me, but I studied poetry in college, so as a control for this experiment, I asked my brother — a blockchain analyst who loves Claude Code and does not read for fun — if he could tell me what the song is about.

sentences.en: If you are not familiar with this song, I urge you to give it a listen, read the lyrics, and think about what it is trying to say.
sentences.zh: 如果你不熟悉这首歌，我强烈建议你听一听，读读歌词，想想它想表达什么。

sentences.en: It seems clear to me, but I studied poetry in college, so as a control for this experiment, I asked my brother — a blockchain analyst who loves Claude Code and does not read for fun — if he could tell me what the song is about.
sentences.zh: 这对我来说似乎很清楚，但我在大学里学过诗歌，所以作为这个实验的对照，我问我的兄弟——一位喜欢克劳德·科德（Claude Code）的区块链分析师，他读书不是为了好玩——他是否可以告诉我这首歌是关于什么的。

blocks.html_en: “I thought climate change at first, then zombie apocalypse, then an asteroid hitting the earth,” he told me. He is correct. It is a song about the human race panicking after learning they will die in a mass-extinction event in five years.

sentences.en: “I thought climate change at first, then zombie apocalypse, then an asteroid hitting the earth,” he told me.
sentences.zh: “我一开始想到的是气候变化，然后是僵尸末日，然后是小行星撞击地球，”他告诉我。

sentences.en: He is correct.
sentences.zh: 他是对的。

sentences.en: It is a song about the human race panicking after learning they will die in a mass-extinction event in five years.
sentences.zh: 这是一首关于人类在得知自己将在五年后的大规模灭绝事件中死亡后的恐慌的歌曲。

sentences.en: Image Credits:Texts from my brother, a consenting participant in this literary experiment
sentences.zh: 图片来源：来自我兄弟的短信，他是这个文学实验的同意参与者

The part of the song that is used for the advertisement was probably chosen because it uses the word “people” over and over again, and without the context of the song, it’s not clear what it’s about.

sentences.zh: 这些人为研究人文学科的价值提出了很好的论据。

sentences.zh: Meta并不是唯一一个在最近的促销活动中出现问题的公司。

sentences.en: Instead of racing to build AGI, the top AI companies seem to be fighting over who can make the creepiest advertisement.
sentences.zh: 顶级人工智能公司似乎没有竞相打造通用人工智能，而是在争夺谁能制作出最令人毛骨悚然的广告。

sentences.en: A few weeks ago, Anthropic released an eerie video of its own.
sentences.zh: 几周前，Anthropic 发布了自己的一段怪异视频。

sentences.en: As my colleague Lucas Ropek described it:
sentences.zh: 正如我的同事卢卡斯·罗佩克 (Lucas Ropek) 所描述的：

blocks.html_en:
The ad begins with a video of a burning house (not exactly a heartwarming start) before pivoting to a series of still images. These images include a crowd of people being surveilled by facial recognition, a homeless person sleeping on the street, rows upon rows of tombstones in a cemetery, and what appears to be a group of laborers toiling in a mine where (presumably) raw materials for smartphones are being dug up.

Meanwhile, a voice-over track features different people asking questions like “Can AI be trusted?” and “Who’s gonna hit the brakes if we need to?”

sentences.en: The ad begins with a video of a burning house (not exactly a heartwarming start) before pivoting to a series of still images.
sentences.zh: 该广告以一段燃烧房屋的视频开始（不完全是一个温馨的开始），然后转向一系列静止图像。

sentences.en: These images include a crowd of people being surveilled by facial recognition, a homeless person sleeping on the street, rows upon rows of tombstones in a cemetery, and what appears to be a group of laborers toiling in a mine where (presumably) raw materials for smartphones are being dug up.
sentences.zh: 这些图像包括一群人受到面部识别的监视、一个无家可归的人睡在街上、墓地里一排排的墓碑，以及一群工人在矿井里辛勤劳作，（大概）正在挖掘智能手机的原材料。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, a voice-over track features different people asking questions like “Can AI be trusted?”
sentences.zh: 与此同时，画外音中不同的人提出诸如“人工智能可以信任吗？”之类的问题。

sentences.en: and “Who’s gonna hit the brakes if we need to?”
sentences.zh: 以及“如果我们需要的话，谁会踩刹车？”

These images include a crowd of people being surveilled by facial recognition, a homeless person sleeping on the street, rows upon rows of tombstones in a cemetery, and what appears to be a group of laborers toiling in a mine where (presumably) raw materials for smartphones are being dug up.

sentences.en: These images include a crowd of people being surveilled by facial recognition, a homeless person sleeping on the street, rows upon rows of tombstones in a cemetery, and what appears to be a group of laborers toiling in a mine where (presumably) raw materials for smartphones are being dug up.

blocks.html_en: Meanwhile, a voice-over track features different people asking questions like “Can AI be trusted?” and “Who’s gonna hit the brakes if we need to?”

sentences.en: Meanwhile, a voice-over track features different people asking questions like “Can AI be trusted?”
sentences.zh: 与此同时，画外音中不同的人提出诸如“人工智能可以信任吗？”之类的问题。

sentences.en: and “Who’s gonna hit the brakes if we need to?”
sentences.zh: 以及“如果我们需要的话，谁会踩刹车？”

blocks.html_en: Anthropic is trying to convince us that it understands the risks AI poses to society, and therefore, this is the company that people can trust to develop AI responsibly. The message it actually conveys feels closer to the mood of Bowie’s “Five Years.”

sentences.en: Anthropic is trying to convince us that it understands the risks AI poses to society, and therefore, this is the company that people can trust to develop AI responsibly.
sentences.zh: Anthropic 试图让我们相信，它了解人工智能给社会带来的风险，因此，这是人们可以信赖的、负责任地开发人工智能的公司。

sentences.en: The message it actually conveys feels closer to the mood of Bowie’s “Five Years.”
sentences.zh: 它实际上传达的信息感觉更接近鲍伊的“五年”的情绪。

blocks.html_en: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman responded to the Anthropic ad, “I thought this was satire, kept looking for the handle to be spelled c1audeai or something.”

sentences.en: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman responded to the Anthropic ad, “I thought this was satire, kept looking for the handle to be spelled c1audeai or something.”
sentences.zh: OpenAI 首席执行官 Sam Altman 回应 Anthropic 广告，“我以为这是讽刺，一直在寻找拼写为 c1audeai 之类的句柄。”

blocks.html_en: As these companies spar to control the public perception of AI, their efforts don’t seem to be making much progress. A recent Pew survey found that only 16% of Americans think that AI’s impact on society over the next 20 years will be positive, and 40% believe it will have a negative impact. Better luck next time, Meta.

sentences.en: As these companies spar to control the public perception of AI, their efforts don’t seem to be making much progress.
sentences.zh: 当这些公司努力控制公众对人工智能的看法时，他们的努力似乎并没有取得太大进展。

sentences.en: A recent Pew survey found that only 16% of Americans think that AI’s impact on society over the next 20 years will be positive, and 40% believe it will have a negative impact.
sentences.zh: 皮尤研究中心最近的一项调查发现，只有 16% 的美国人认为人工智能在未来 20 年对社会的影响将是积极的，40% 的人认为它将产生负面影响。

sentences.en: Better luck next time, Meta.
sentences.zh: 下次祝你好运，梅塔。

blocks.html_en: Amanda Silberling is a senior writer at TechCrunch covering the intersection of technology and culture. She has also written for publications like Polygon, MTV, the Kenyon Review, NPR, and Business Insider. She is the co-host of Wow If True, a podcast about internet culture, with science fiction author Isabel J. Kim. Prior to joining TechCrunch, she worked as a grassroots organizer, museum educator, and film festival coordinator. She holds a B.A. in English from the University of Pennsylvania and served as a Princeton in Asia Fellow in Laos.

blocks.html_en: View Bio

sentences.en: View Bio
sentences.zh: 查看简历

content_html_zh:
Meta 的最新广告以黑白镜头开始，向我们展示了人们在阅读无数关于人工智能将如何抢走我们的工作、孤立我们并引发全球危机的恐慌头条新闻时所看到的情况。

“有些人会让你相信人工智能会让我们感觉联系更少。 它会把我们抛在后面，”画外音说道。 “我们完全不同意。”

突然，视频变成了彩色，并显示了不同的人睁开眼睛微笑的循环。 然后，我们看到一对夫妇在屋顶上跳舞，指着彩虹；一群青少年在湖里游泳；一个孩子在田野里嬉戏；朋友们分开一段时间后拥抱。

“我们是乐观主义者。 请称我们为梦想家。 不管你想要什么，给我们打电话。 但我们把赌注押在人身上，我们喜欢这些赔率，”画外音说道。 “未来属于每个人。”

这是一种很好的情绪，对于一家投入数千亿美元押注人工智能将彻底改变人类的公司来说也相当方便。 但广告中最奇怪的部分并不是我们通过 Instagram 帖子观看这些快乐的时刻。 广告的配乐是大卫·鲍伊的歌曲《五年》。

如果你不熟悉这首歌，我强烈建议你听一听，读读歌词，想想它想表达什么。 这对我来说似乎很清楚，但我在大学里学过诗歌，所以作为这个实验的对照，我问我的兄弟——一位喜欢克劳德·科德（Claude Code）的区块链分析师，他读书不是为了好玩——他是否可以告诉我这首歌是关于什么的。

“我一开始想到的是气候变化，然后是僵尸末日，然后是小行星撞击地球，”他告诉我。 他是对的。 这是一首关于人类在得知自己将在五年后的大规模灭绝事件中死亡后的恐慌的歌曲。

如果您不熟悉鲍伊的音乐，这首歌可能听起来快乐且鼓舞人心，与广告的乐观基调相匹配。 选择歌曲中用于广告的部分可能是因为它一遍又一遍地使用“人”这个词，并且没有歌曲的上下文，不清楚它是关于什么的。

但是如果我们看一下本节之前的几行：

新闻刚刚传来，我们还剩下五年的时间来哭泣新闻人哭着告诉我们地球真的快死了，哭得脸都湿了然后我知道他没有说谎

我们“还有五年的时间可以哭泣”，“地球真的快要死了”。 这是相当惨淡的。

在播放一首有关世界末日的歌曲时，让人们相信人工智能将使世界变得更美好并不令人放心，然而，这种矛盾的音乐选择似乎已经绕过了 Meta 的每个人，包括首席执行官马克·扎克伯格。

“Meta 始终相信赋予人们以你想要的方式分享、联系和塑造你的世界的能力，”他在视频旁边写道。 “当我们进入人工智能的下一波浪潮时，我们仍然相信未来属于每个人。 我们致力于为每个人提供充分发挥潜力的工具，并确保每个人都能受益于技术。”

话又说回来，技术领导者并不以文学分析能力而闻名。 Meta 的 Oculus 过去常常向新员工发放科幻小说《头号玩家》(Ready Player One)，该小说的故事背景是一个反乌托邦，一家生产虚拟现实产品的科技公司变得过于强大和邪恶。 OpenAI 首席执行官 Sam Altman 直接引用了电影《她》的灵感，这部电影警告我们，当我们使用人工智能进行情感支持时可能会出现问题。 Palantir 是一家为政府构建人工智能监控系统的公司，以 Palantir 命名，Palantir 是《指环王》系列中的一颗视觉宝石，黑魔王用它来监视敌人。 埃隆·马斯克目前正在对克里斯托弗·诺兰改编的大片《奥德赛》的“准确性”大发雷霆，该故事包含海怪、魔法和神圣干预等现实元素。 这些人为研究人文学科的价值提出了很好的论据。

Meta并不是唯一一个在最近的促销活动中出现问题的公司。 顶级人工智能公司似乎没有竞相打造通用人工智能，而是在争夺谁能制作出最令人毛骨悚然的广告。 几周前，Anthropic 发布了自己的一段怪异视频。 正如我的同事卢卡斯·罗佩克 (Lucas Ropek) 所描述的：

该广告以一段燃烧房屋的视频开始（不完全是一个温馨的开始），然后转向一系列静止图像。 这些图像包括一群人受到面部识别的监视、一个无家可归的人睡在街上、墓地里一排排的墓碑，以及一群工人在矿井里辛勤劳作，（大概）正在挖掘智能手机的原材料。 与此同时，画外音中不同的人提出诸如“人工智能可以信任吗？”之类的问题。 以及“如果我们需要的话，谁会踩刹车？”

Anthropic 试图让我们相信，它了解人工智能给社会带来的风险，因此，这是人们可以信赖的、负责任地开发人工智能的公司。 它实际上传达的信息感觉更接近鲍伊的“五年”的情绪。

OpenAI 首席执行官 Sam Altman 回应 Anthropic 广告，“我以为这是讽刺，一直在寻找拼写为 c1audeai 之类的句柄。”

当这些公司努力控制公众对人工智能的看法时，他们的努力似乎并没有取得太大进展。 皮尤研究中心最近的一项调查发现，只有 16% 的美国人认为人工智能在未来 20 年对社会的影响将是积极的，40% 的人认为它将产生负面影响。 下次祝你好运，梅塔。

当您通过我们文章中的链接购买时，我们可能会赚取少量佣金。 这并不影响我们的编辑独立性。

Amanda Silberling 是 TechCrunch 的资深撰稿人，内容涉及技术与文化的交叉点。 她还为 Polygon、MTV、Kenyon Review、NPR 和 Business Insider 等出版物撰稿。 她与科幻小说作家 Isabel J. 共同主持了有关网络文化的播客 Wow If True。 金. 在加入 TechCrunch 之前，她曾担任基层组织者、博物馆教育家和电影节协调员。 她拥有学士学位。 他拥有宾夕法尼亚大学英语学士学位，并曾在老挝担任普林斯顿亚洲研究员。

您可以通过发送电子邮件至 amanda@techcrunch.com 或通过 Signal 上的@amanda.100 发送加密消息来联系或验证 Amanda 的联系。
